subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 12! In this unit you will master ratio and proportion, two of the most useful ideas in all of mathematics. A ratio is a way of comparing quantities, and a proportion tells us how one quantity relates to another as they grow or shrink together. These ideas are hiding everywhere in daily life. When Amina mixes three scoops of flour with two scoops of sugar to make anjero, she is using a ratio. When Yusuf doubles a recipe to feed twice as many guests at iftar, he is using proportion. When Halima reads a map to work out how far Hargeisa is from Berbera, she is using a scale, which is simply a ratio in disguise.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the standardized Cambridge Stage 6 workbook source rows for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, work through every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky. Nothing here has to be rushed. Understanding deeply is far more valuable than racing to the end.

Why does this matter so much? Proportional reasoning is one of the great turning points in a young mathematician's life. Traders in the Eastleigh market use ratios to work out fair prices and change. Farmers near Kismaayo use proportion to mix animal feed and to share the profit from selling goats and camels. Builders use scale drawings before they lay a single stone. Families use proportion to share food and money fairly. Once you can think proportionally, you begin to see the hidden mathematical structure inside dozens of everyday situations, and you gain a tool you will use for the rest of your life.

As you work, keep a pencil and paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading; you must do it. Draw the bar models, write out the ratios, and say the relationships aloud. If you make a mistake, do not be discouraged; a mistake is simply a signpost showing exactly what to practise next. Every skilled mathematician was once a beginner working through these very same ideas, one careful step at a time.
Understand what a ratio is and write it in words, with a colon, and as a fraction.
Explain why order matters in a ratio and tell apart part-to-part and part-to-whole comparisons.
Find equivalent ratios by multiplying or dividing both parts by the same number.
Simplify a ratio to its simplest form using the greatest common factor.
Share a quantity in a given ratio, including three-part ratios.
Recognise direct proportion and solve proportion problems using unit rates, equivalent ratios, and cross-multiplication.
Use scale to solve problems about maps, plans and models.

concepts.title: What a Ratio Is and How to Write It
concepts.explanation: A ratio is a way of comparing two or more quantities of the same kind. It tells you how much of one thing there is compared with another. Think of Khadija making green paint for a school mural. She mixes 3 parts blue with 1 part yellow. The ratio of blue to yellow is 3:1, said aloud as 'three to one'. This does not tell us exactly how many litres she uses; it tells us the relationship between the amounts. She could use 3 litres and 1 litre, or 6 litres and 2 litres, and the colour would come out exactly the same…
concepts.example: In words: 6 to 4, or '6 mangoes to 4 bananas'.

concepts.title: Equivalent Ratios
concepts.explanation: Equivalent ratios are different ratios that describe exactly the same relationship. They work just like equivalent fractions. If Maryam mixes camel milk and water in the ratio 2:3, she gets the same drink whether she uses a small jug or a large pot, as long as the relationship stays the same. So 2:3, 4:6, 6:9 and 8:12 are all equivalent; they all mean 'three parts water for every two parts milk'. You find an equivalent ratio by multiplying or dividing both parts by the same number. This is the golden rule:…

concepts.title: Simplifying Ratios to Simplest Form
concepts.explanation: The simplest form of a ratio uses the smallest possible whole numbers while keeping the same relationship. Simplifying a ratio is like tidying a room: nothing important changes, but everything becomes clearer. Just as we simplify fractions, we simplify a ratio by dividing both parts by their greatest common factor, the largest number that divides into both. List the factors of each number. Factors of 12: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12. Factors of 18: 1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18.
concepts.example: Find the common factors: 1, 2, 3 and 6. The greatest of these is 6.

concepts.title: Sharing an Amount in a Given Ratio
concepts.explanation: One of the most useful skills in this unit is sharing a quantity in a given ratio. This comes up whenever something has to be divided fairly but not equally. Suppose three business partners in Mombasa, Idris, Zahra and Abdi, share a profit of 20 000 shillings in the ratio 2:3:5. How much does each receive? The method is always the same, and it rests on one clever idea: first find the value of a single part. Add the ratio numbers to find the total number of parts: 2 + 3 + 5 = 10 parts.
concepts.example: Divide the total amount by the number of parts to find the value of one part: 20 000 ÷ 10 = 2 000 shillings per part.

concepts.title: Direct Proportion
concepts.explanation: Two quantities are in direct proportion when they grow and shrink together at the same rate, so that their ratio stays constant. If one doubles, the other doubles; if one halves, the other halves. A clear example is work and pay. If Liban earns 10 shillings per hour picking mangoes, then: The ratio of hours to shillings is always 1:10, no matter how many hours he works. That constant ratio is the signature of direct proportion. Another everyday example is distance and time at a steady speed. A bus travelling from…
concepts.example: How can you tell whether two quantities really are in direct proportion? There are two clear tests. First, when one quantity is zero, the other must also be zero; if Liban works no hours, he earns no pay. Second, the…

concepts.title: Three Methods for Solving Proportion Problems
concepts.explanation: When two quantities are in direct proportion and you know three of the four values, you can always find the missing one. There are three trusted methods, and it is worth knowing all three so you can choose the easiest for each problem. The unit rate is the amount for exactly one unit. Finding it first often makes everything else simple. Suppose 3 pencils cost £1.50. How much do 8 pencils cost? First find the cost of one pencil: £1.50 ÷ 3 = £0.50 per pencil. Then multiply by 8: 8 × £0.50 = £4.00. The unit rate is…

methods.title: How to simplify a ratio
methods.example: Write the ratio 24:36 in its simplest form.
steps: Find the greatest common factor of both parts.
Divide each part by that factor.
Check that no number except 1 divides into both parts.

methods.title: How to solve a proportion
methods.example: A paint mixture uses blue and yellow in the ratio 4:3. If you use 12 litres of blue, how much yellow do you need?
steps: Write the two ratios, keeping matching quantities lined up.
Choose a method: unit rate, scale factor, or cross-multiplication.
Calculate the missing value.
Check your answer using a second method or by simplifying.

methods.title: How to share in a ratio
methods.example: Share £120 between Amina and Hassan in the ratio 3:5.
steps: Add the parts of the ratio to get the total number of parts.
Divide the amount by the total parts to find one part.
Multiply each ratio number by the value of one part.
Add the shares back to confirm they equal the original total.

methods.title: Direct proportion with the unit rate
methods.example: If 3 sambusas cost 45 shillings, how much do 8 sambusas cost?
steps: Solution: Find the unit rate: 45 ÷ 3 = 15 shillings per sambusa.
Then 8 × 15 = 120 shillings for 8 sambusas.
Check the direct proportion with the unit rate result carefully.

methods.title: Proportion by cross-multiplication
methods.example: If 5 pencils cost £2.50, how much do 8 pencils cost?
steps: Solution: Write 5/2.50 = 8/x.
Cross-multiply: 5 × x = 2.50 × 8, so 5x = 20.
(Check with the unit rate: £2.50 ÷ 5 = £0.50 each, and 8 × £0.50 = £4.00.

methods.title: A map scale
methods.example: On a map with scale 1:25 000, two masjids are 6 cm apart. What is the real distance?
steps: Solution: 1 cm represents 25 000 cm.
So 6 cm represents 6 × 25 000 = 150 000 cm.
Convert: 150 000 cm = 1 500 m = 1.5 km.

workedExamples.title: Simplify a ratio
workedExamples.prompt: Write the ratio 24:36 in its simplest form.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Find the greatest common factor of 24 and 36. The factors of 24 include 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24 and the factors of 36 include 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 9, 12, 18, 36. The greatest common factor is 12. Divide both parts: 24 ÷ 12 = 2 and 36 ÷ 12 = 3. So 24:36 = 2:3.

workedExamples.title: Find a missing equivalent ratio
workedExamples.prompt: A paint mixture uses blue and yellow in the ratio 4:3. If you use 12 litres of blue, how much yellow do you need?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The scale factor from 4 to 12 is 12 ÷ 4 = 3. Apply the same factor to the yellow: 3 × 3 = 9 litres of yellow. Check the ratio: 12:9 simplifies to 4:3, exactly as it should.

workedExamples.title: Share in a ratio
workedExamples.prompt: Share £120 between Amina and Hassan in the ratio 3:5.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Total parts = 3 + 5 = 8. One part = £120 ÷ 8 = £15. Amina gets 3 × £15 = £45; Hassan gets 5 × £15 = £75. Check: £45 + £75 = £120. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Direct proportion with the unit rate
workedExamples.prompt: If 3 sambusas cost 45 shillings, how much do 8 sambusas cost?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Find the unit rate: 45 ÷ 3 = 15 shillings per sambusa. Then 8 × 15 = 120 shillings for 8 sambusas.

workedExamples.title: Proportion by cross-multiplication
workedExamples.prompt: If 5 pencils cost £2.50, how much do 8 pencils cost?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Write 5/2.50 = 8/x. Cross-multiply: 5 × x = 2.50 × 8, so 5x = 20. Then x = 20 ÷ 5 = £4.00. (Check with the unit rate: £2.50 ÷ 5 = £0.50 each, and 8 × £0.50 = £4.00. The answers agree.)

workedExamples.title: A map scale
workedExamples.prompt: On a map with scale 1:25 000, two masjids are 6 cm apart. What is the real distance?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 1 cm represents 25 000 cm. So 6 cm represents 6 × 25 000 = 150 000 cm. Convert: 150 000 cm = 1 500 m = 1.5 km.

workedExamples.title: A three-part ratio
workedExamples.prompt: To make a colour, red, blue and yellow paint are mixed in the ratio 4:3:2. How much of each is needed for 180 ml of mixture?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Total parts = 4 + 3 + 2 = 9. One part = 180 ÷ 9 = 20 ml. Red = 4 × 20 = 80 ml; blue = 3 × 20 = 60 ml; yellow = 2 × 20 = 40 ml. Check: 80 + 60 + 40 = 180 ml. Correct.

workedExamples.title: A percentage as proportion
workedExamples.prompt: In a survey of 200 people in Garissa, the ratio of those who prefer tea to those who prefer coffee is 3:2. How many prefer each drink?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Total parts = 3 + 2 = 5. One part = 200 ÷ 5 = 40 people. Tea = 3 × 40 = 120 people; coffee = 2 × 40 = 80 people. Check: 120 + 80 = 200. As percentages, that is 60% tea and 40% coffee.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 9
workedExamples.prompt: If 5 pencils cost £2.50, how much do 8 pencils cost? Use the unit rate.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 10
workedExamples.prompt: Calculate each unit rate: a) 240 km in 4 hours b) £45 for 9 items c) 150 pages in 6 days.
workedExamples.solution: a) 60 km/h, b) £5 per item, c) 25 pages per day.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 11
workedExamples.prompt: A car travels 240 km in 3 hours at a steady speed. At this rate, how far will it travel in 5 hours?

workedExamples.title: Guided example 12
workedExamples.prompt: Share £4 800 between three people in the ratio 3:4:5. Show your working and check your answer.

activities.title: The Ratio Kitchen
activities.materials: cups or spoons, and ingredients such as flour, sugar or rice.
steps: Choose a simple mixture, for example 3 cups of flour to 2 cups of sugar (a 3:2 ratio).
Measure out the mixture and write the ratio in colon form.
Now scale the recipe up to feed twice as many people. Double both amounts and write the new ratio.
Scale it down by half. What do you notice about all three ratios?
You should find that 3:2, 6:4 and 1.5:1 are all equivalent; the taste stays the same because the ratio stays the same.

activities.title: Colour Mixing Laboratory
activities.materials: paints or coloured water, small cups, and a dropper or spoon.
steps: Mix two colours in a chosen ratio, for example 3 drops blue to 1 drop yellow.
Record the ratio and the colour you made.
Make a second batch using an equivalent ratio, such as 6 drops to 2 drops.
Check that both batches give the same shade. Then try changing the ratio and watch the colour change.
This shows that equivalent ratios really do keep the relationship, and therefore the colour, exactly the same.

activities.title: The Fair Share Game
activities.materials: a handful of dates, stones or bottle caps, and two or more players.
steps: Count out a total, for example 24 dates.
Choose a sharing ratio, such as 3:5, for two players.
Work out the shares by finding the value of one part first (24 ÷ 8 = 3 per part).
Hand out the shares (9 and 15) and check they add back to the total. Play again with a three-part ratio.
This game trains the 'find one part first' method until it becomes automatic.

activities.title: Map and Scale Investigation
activities.materials: a map (paper or on a screen you can measure), a ruler, and paper.
steps: Find the scale of your map, for example 1:50 000.
Measure the distance between two places in centimetres.
Use the scale to work out the real distance, converting to kilometres.
Plan a pretend journey between two East African cities and calculate the real distance from the map.

activities.title: Best-Value Detective (Shopping Investigation)
activities.materials: prices of items in different pack sizes (real packets or made-up prices).
steps: Choose an item sold in two different pack sizes, for example 6 for £3 and 10 for £4.50.
Work out the unit rate (the price of one) for each pack.
Decide which pack is better value and explain why using the unit rates.
Make a shopping guide comparing the best value for three different items.

activities.title: Body Proportion Study (Family Task)
activities.materials: a tape measure or string, and family members to measure.
steps: Measure each person's height and their arm span (fingertip to fingertip).
Write the ratio of arm span to height for each person and simplify it.
Compare the ratios. Are they roughly the same for everyone in the family?
Discuss what you found. Many people have an arm span close to their height, a ratio near 1:1.

After your activities, talk or write about these questions: Which activity helped you understand ratio and proportion best? What surprised you about equivalent ratios or scale? Where did you notice ratios hiding in everyday life? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: The single most important rule about ratios is that order matters. The ratio of mangoes to bananas is 6:4, but the ratio of bananas to mangoes is 4:6. These are not the same statement, just as 'six goats and four camels' is a different picture from 'four goats and six camels'. The first number always matches the first quantity you name. Whenever you write a ratio, name the quantities in order and keep the numbers in the same order.

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: You find an equivalent ratio by multiplying or dividing both parts by the same number. This is the golden rule: whatever you do to one part, you must do to the other. Starting from 3:5:

terms: A comparison of two or more quantities of the same kind
Different ratios showing the same relationship
A ratio using the smallest whole numbers
The greatest common factor of the parts
A statement that two ratios are equal
Quantities that grow and shrink at the same rate
The amount for exactly one unit
A ratio comparing a drawing or model to real life
commonMistakes: Keep numbers in the order you name the quantities
Multiply or divide both parts by the same number
Divide both parts by the GCF to get 2:3
Read whether the question wants part-to-part or part-to-whole
Add the shares back to the original total

realProblems.prompt: A cake recipe serves 8 people and uses 400 g flour, 300 g sugar, 6 eggs and 250 ml milk. Convert the recipe for 12 people.
realProblems.answer: 1) Scale factor 12 ÷ 8 = 1.5, so 600 g flour, 450 g sugar, 9 eggs and 375 ml milk.

questions.question: What does “Ratio” mean?
options: A comparison of two or more quantities of the same kind
Different ratios showing the same relationship
A ratio using the smallest whole numbers
The greatest common factor of the parts
questions.answer: A comparison of two or more quantities of the same kind
questions.hint: Use the Unit 12 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Ratio means A comparison of two or more quantities of the same kind.

questions.question: What does “Equivalent ratios” mean?
options: Different ratios showing the same relationship
A ratio using the smallest whole numbers
The greatest common factor of the parts

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, work through every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky. Nothing here has to be rushed. Understanding deeply is far more valuable than racing to the end.

I can why does this matter so much? Proportional reasoning is one of the great turning points in a young mathematician's life. Traders in the Eastleigh market use ratios to work out fair prices and change. Farmers near Kismaayo use proportion to mix animal feed and to share the profit from selling goats and camels. Builders use scale drawings before they lay a single stone. Families use proportion to share food and money fairly. Once you can think proportionally, you begin to see the hidden mathematical structure inside dozens of everyday situations, and you gain a tool you will use for the rest of your life.

I can as you work, keep a pencil and paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading; you must do it. Draw the bar models, write out the ratios, and say the relationships aloud. If you make a mistake, do not be discouraged; a mistake is simply a signpost showing exactly what to practise next. Every skilled mathematician was once a beginner working through these very same ideas, one careful step at a time.
I can understand what a ratio is and write it in words, with a colon, and as a fraction.
I can explain why order matters in a ratio and tell apart part-to-part and part-to-whole comparisons.
I can find equivalent ratios by multiplying or dividing both parts by the same number.
I can simplify a ratio to its simplest form using the greatest common factor.
I can share a quantity in a given ratio, including three-part ratios.
